item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have decided to just restore the original version. I can't get it right, it may as well remain as it was originally intended. Might touch it up at some point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have decided to edit this again, and cut out the interview entirely. I think the idea of Margaret disappearing mysteriously is a good note to end this surreal thing on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since the authorship is no longer clear from this: this skip was written by me, Tufto, on my first account prior to my deleting it. You can find more of my stuff here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have made some substantial edits to this page. The later logs are mostly cut, and U90-3's arc is made far more coherent. I think this version is better; it reduces some of the needless waffle which it had in the first version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So the Glowball's power is, it takes you where you want to go, but first it makes you want to go to unintelligible nonsense?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me a lot of Narnia.

I agree with other people about the dialogue and narrative. Particularly the fact that the convo between the two Margaret's is supposed to be transcribed from a radio transmission - it doesn't really feel that way .

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Strongly echoing Shaggy's thought above. The imagery and world-building of the frontier was strong, but not strong enough to make me upvote-which I imagine is okay, because this article isn't trying to carry itself on imagery or world-building alone. The problem for me is that I felt like the narrative arc was much worse than the first half of the article. In addition to the points that Shaggy raises, the dialogue between the two Margarets felt stilted-you're not doing yourself any favors when it comes to writing believable dialogue when the conversation also has to world-build, and I think you're also way under-using the potential of the conversation-with-the-self (which is, uh, a narrative technique I particularly enjoy). I also have no idea what the stakes of the final choice are and I have no idea what Margaret's motivation is in making her final choice. I also also was thrown way the hell out of the article by the "Hey Foundation are you there" joke, lampshading notwithstanding. Balances out to a neutral vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I admit that I'm a bit disappointed that the cactus didn't do podcasts instead.

at least make it a dj ;-;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
potatolos

I thought you were joking.

Apparently not.

In all seriousness, I have to echo Shaggy's statement above - the worldbuilding is fantastic and really immersive. Great job! +1 :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From the first line of the description and up on through the table describing the MTF's findings, I was blown away by how remarkably well-crafted this world is. This earned an upvote five time over (though, I'm of the opinion that the Humes nonsense is unnecessary, it can just be odd and working by on its own set of logic).

I think there's some improvement to be had still in Swan's character development/arc. The first interview with herself seems like more exposition on the setting than establishing who she is as a character. This issue seems to continue into the next log as well, so by the ending, I'm at a loss as to what she's doing, where she intends to go, what's motivating her, and why anything she's doing matters in the slightest. It's thus very hard for me to get invested in her journey or care about her, and had I not been so enthralled by the setting, I'd have downvoted for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For the record, I still think that a mass transport would be more suitable and manageable (although that would align more to a 'settlement' theme, as opposed to an 'exploration' theme).

Notably, this means that the MTFs involved have to learn how to get on horses they can't touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh hey, didn't expect to see this posted so quick!

I really have no further comments beyond what I told you in chat- the method by which they enter the dimension is very unique, the descriptions of the organisms and events was a blast and really made the universe feel more fleshed-out, and all in all it was a lovely ride. +1

I'm very glad to have had my small part in helping this succeed. Very, very good work. :^)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is surprisingly well done. The anomaly itself didn't caught me but it was well written that I still upvoted. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Still pondering whether I like this enough to upvote, but I have to salute this:

and the use of a species of singing cactus as a form of public entertainment

Do they still have Mondays off?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The link's wrong—it says 2865, but it goes to 2895.
Otherwise, fantastic job. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The paragraph about Humes almost made me reach for the downvote button - and the sentence about the organisms living in the dimension absolutely does not belong there - but I stuck it out and enjoyed the journey through the desert in between the worlds.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With thanks to:

MrWrong
Modern_Erasmus
Dr0Shadow
bitter

I tried to edit more faithfully according to the suggestions and criticisms of my critiquers this time; hopefully this will have paid off.
